regulations.name: General Data Protection Regulation
regulations.authority: European Data Protection Board (EDPB)
regulations.summary: The world's most comprehensive data protection law, governing how organizations collect, store, process, and transfer personal data of individuals in the EU. It applies to any organization worldwide that handles EU resident data, regardless of where the organization is based.

key_requirements.text: Establish and document a lawful basis for every personal data processing activity
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful processing; fines up to €20M or 4% global annual turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Records of Processing Activities (RoPA); legitimate interest assessments; consent records

key_requirements.text: Honour all data subject rights: access (Art.15), erasure (Art.17), portability (Art.20), rectification (Art.16), restriction (Art.18), objection (Art.21)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory authority enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DSR handling procedures; documented response timelines; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Report personal data breaches to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; possible criminal liability in some member states
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach notification log; incident response runbook; DPA submission receipts

key_requirements.text: Notify affected data subjects without undue delay when a breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights and freedoms
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; reputational and legal exposure
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach notification letters; risk assessment records; communication logs

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) for public authorities, large-scale systematic monitoring, or large-scale sensitive data processing
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; inability to demonstrate accountability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DPO appointment letter; DPO contract or job description; registration with supervisory authority

key_requirements.text: Implement Privacy by Design and Privacy by Default in all systems and processes involving personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory remediation orders by regulators
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy impact review in SDLC; default settings documentation; design decisions log

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) before commencing high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; enforcement action; processing suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Completed DPIA templates; evidence of prior consultation with DPA where required

key_requirements.text: Ensure personal data is not transferred outside the EEA without an adequate safeguard (adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs, or derogation)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €20M or 4% global turnover; injunctions on data flows
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Transfer impact assessments; executed SCCs; adequacy decision references; BCR approvals

key_requirements.text: Enter into binding Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with all processors handling personal data on your behalf
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; joint liability exposure with processors
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Signed DPA index; processor audit reports; sub-processor approval records

key_requirements.text: Maintain Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) as required by Article 30
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory authority audit findings
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Up-to-date RoPA spreadsheet or register; review date and owner records

regulations.penalties: Up to 20 million EUR or 4% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher)

milestones.label: EDPB coordinated enforcement on right of access

regulations.name: Network and Information Security Directive 2
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A landmark EU directive that significantly expands cybersecurity obligations for organizations operating critical infrastructure and essential services. NIS2 broadens the scope of its predecessor to include more sectors and introduces stricter requirements for risk management, incident reporting, and supply chain security.

key_requirements.text: Implement cybersecurity risk management measures: policies, incident handling, supply chain security, network security, encryption, MFA, access control
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; operational suspension orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Cybersecurity policy; risk register; ISO 27001 or equivalent audit; MFA deployment records

key_requirements.text: Report significant incidents to national CSIRT/NCA within 24 hours (early warning) and 72 hours (notification)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €10M or 2% global turnover; public disclosure requirements
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident log; CSIRT notification receipts; post-incident analysis reports

key_requirements.text: Ensure supply chain cybersecurity: assess security practices of direct suppliers and service providers
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory orders to remediate third-party risks
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Supplier security assessment questionnaires; contract security clauses; audit rights

key_requirements.text: Management body must receive training in cybersecurity risk management and oversee its implementation
key_requirements.consequence: Personal liability of management; fines for organisation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/management training records; board cybersecurity agenda items; meeting minutes

key_requirements.text: Register with the national competent authority and provide required organisational information
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; non-compliant operation of essential/important entity
key_requirements.evidence_guide: NCA registration confirmation; entity classification documentation

key_requirements.text: Test incident response and business continuity plans regularly through exercises and drills
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; inability to demonstrate resilience capability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Table-top exercise reports; drill schedules; BCP test results; lessons-learned records

regulations.penalties: Up to 10 million EUR or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities

regulations.name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
regulations.authority: European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, ESMA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that establishes uniform requirements for the digital operational resilience of financial entities in the EU. DORA consolidates and upgrades ICT risk requirements previously scattered across multiple sectoral regulations, specifically for banks, insurers, investment firms, and their critical ICT third-party providers.

key_requirements.text: Establish an ICT risk management framework with board-approved policies, risk appetite, and governance structure
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; supervisory orders; management personal liability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT risk management framework document; board approval minutes; risk appetite statement

key_requirements.text: Classify and report major ICT-related incidents to competent authority within prescribed timelines (initial: 4 hours, intermediate: 72 hours, final: 1 month)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 1% average daily worldwide turnover (banks); supervisory scrutiny
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT incident classification methodology; incident log; NCA submission receipts

key_requirements.text: Conduct Digital Operational Resilience Testing (DORT) annually — basic testing and TLPT for significant entities
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory finding; mandatory remediation; fines
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Annual test plan; penetration test reports; TLPT scoping documentation; remediation tracker

key_requirements.text: Manage ICT third-party risk through rigorous pre-contractual due diligence, contract provisions, and ongoing monitoring
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory action; withdrawal of authorisation; fines
key_requirements.evidence_guide: TPPP (Third-Party Provider Policy); DDQ responses; contract review records; exit plans

key_requirements.text: Ensure contractual provisions with ICT third-party providers include: SLAs, audit rights, exit strategies, data portability, incident cooperation
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory contract renegotiation; supervisory orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT contract register; contract review checklist; executed contract clauses

key_requirements.text: Maintain an updated register of all ICT third-party arrangements and report concentration risks
key_requirements.consequence: Supervisory findings; fines; forced diversification
key_requirements.evidence_guide: ICT third-party register; critical third-party assessment; concentration risk analysis

regulations.penalties: Up to 2% of global annual turnover; periodic penalty payments up to 1% of average daily turnover

milestones.label: Full application date — ICT risk, incident reporting, testing, third-party risk
First round of TLPT (Threat-Led Pen Testing) required

regulations.name: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
regulations.authority: EU AI Office (European Commission)
regulations.summary: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act is the world's first comprehensive AI regulation, entered into force on 1 August 2024. It applies a risk-based approach: prohibited AI practices banned from 2 February 2025; rules for General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models apply from 2 August 2025; high-risk AI system requirements apply from 2 August 2026. The AI Act covers providers, deployers, and importers of AI systems in the EU/EEA regardless of where the provider is established.

key_requirements.text: Identify and cease any use of prohibited AI systems (subliminal manipulation, social scoring, real-time remote biometric ID in public spaces without exception)
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €35M or 7% global annual turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: AI system inventory; prohibited AI classification assessment; decommission records

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI systems: establish a risk management system and conduct a conformity assessment before market placement or putting into service
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €15M or 3% global annual turnover; market withdrawal orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk management system documentation; conformity assessment report; EU database registration

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI: implement data governance practices for training, validation, and testing datasets including bias testing
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; mandatory withdrawal from market
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Dataset documentation; bias testing reports; data quality assessments

key_requirements.text: For high-risk AI: maintain technical documentation, generate logs automatically, and enable meaningful human oversight
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; market access restrictions; supervisory investigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Technical documentation file; logging configuration; human oversight procedures

key_requirements.text: GPAI model providers must comply with transparency obligations, copyright law, and publish training data summaries
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €15M or 3% global turnover; systemic risk obligations for frontier models
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Model technical documentation; training data summary; copyright compliance assessment

key_requirements.text: Disclose to users when they are interacting with an AI system unless the context makes it obvious
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to €7.5M or 1.5% global turnover
key_requirements.evidence_guide: AI disclosure notices; chatbot/voice bot disclosure logs; UI design evidence

regulations.penalties: Up to 35 million EUR or 7% of global annual turnover for prohibited AI violations

milestones.label: All high-risk AI (Annex III) obligations apply

regulations.name: EU Cybersecurity Act
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that grants ENISA a permanent mandate and establishes an EU-wide cybersecurity certification framework for ICT products, services, and processes.

key_requirements.text: Voluntary European cybersecurity certification schemes for ICT products, services, and processes
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Three assurance levels: basic, substantial, and high based on risk profile
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Manufacturers can self-declare conformity at the basic level; independent assessment required for high level
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: National certification supervisory authorities must be designated by each member state
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: ENISA maintains a database of all issued, amended, and withdrawn certificates
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Cybersecurity certification does not replace sector-specific requirements (e.g., NIS2, DORA)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

regulations.penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims

regulations.name: California Consumer Privacy Act (as amended by CPRA)
regulations.authority: California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA)
regulations.summary: California's comprehensive privacy law giving residents broad rights over their personal information. Considered the most stringent U.S. state privacy law and a model for other states.

key_requirements.text: Disclose categories of personal information collected and purposes at or before point of collection
key_requirements.consequence: AG enforcement; $2,500/unintentional or $7,500/intentional violation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy notice; collection point disclosures; consent forms; website audit

key_requirements.text: Respond to consumer rights requests (know, delete, opt-out of sale, non-discrimination) within 45 days
key_requirements.consequence: Enforcement by CA AG; private right of action for security breaches
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consumer request logs; response time records; request fulfilment documentation

key_requirements.text: Provide a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" opt-out mechanism
key_requirements.consequence: $7,500 per intentional violation; AG enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Opt-out link on website; opt-out request logs; downstream vendor notification records

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect personal information
key_requirements.consequence: Private right of action for breaches; AG enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security risk assessment; NIST CSF or ISO 27001 mapping; pen test reports; incident logs

key_requirements.text: Update Privacy Policy at least every 12 months to reflect current practices
key_requirements.consequence: AG enforcement; consumer complaints
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Privacy policy version history; legal review records; publication confirmation

regulations.penalties: Up to $2,500 per unintentional violation; $7,500 per intentional violation

regulations.name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) - Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing national standards for protecting the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI). Applies to covered entities and business associates.

key_requirements.text: Conduct an accurate and thorough risk analysis of potential risks and vulnerabilities to ePHI
key_requirements.consequence: Civil penalties up to $1.9M per category per year; criminal prosecution
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk analysis report; asset inventory; threat assessment documentation; annual review records

key_requirements.text: Implement risk management measures to reduce identified risks to a reasonable and appropriate level
key_requirements.consequence: Civil money penalties; mandatory corrective action plan
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk management plan; remediation tracking; control implementation evidence

key_requirements.text: Implement access controls limiting ePHI access to authorized persons and software programs only
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; corrective action plan; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; active directory / IAM audit reports; least-privilege reviews

key_requirements.text: Implement audit controls to record and examine activity on systems containing ePHI
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; inability to detect or investigate breaches
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Audit log configuration; log retention policy; SIEM alerts; periodic log review records

key_requirements.text: Notify affected individuals, HHS, and (where applicable) media within required timeframes following a breach of unsecured PHI
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties up to $1.9M per incident category; OCR investigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach assessment log; HHS notification receipts; individual notification records

key_requirements.text: Provide patients with Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) describing how PHI is used and disclosed
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; complaints to OCR
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Current NPP document; distribution records; website posting evidence

key_requirements.text: Enter into Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with all vendors who access, create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI on your behalf
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; joint liability; OCR enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Executed BAA index; vendor assessment records; BAA review dates

key_requirements.text: Train all workforce members on HIPAA policies and procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Penalties; inability to invoke workforce training defence
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; training materials; assessment scores; annual refresh evidence

regulations.penalties: Tiered: $100-$50,000 per violation; maximum $1.9 million per violation category per year

milestones.label: HIPAA Security Rule NPRM published — major update
Proposed updated Security Rule compliance deadline

regulations.name: Sarbanes-Oxley Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing enhanced standards for all U.S. publicly traded companies and their auditors, focused on financial reporting integrity and internal controls.

key_requirements.text: CEO and CFO must personally certify accuracy of financial statements (Section 302)
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal penalties up to $5M and 20 years imprisonment for false certifications
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Signed Section 302 certifications; sub-certifications from business unit leaders

key_requirements.text: Assess and report on effectiveness of internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) annually (Section 404)
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; restatements; shareholder litigation; delisting risk
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Management assessment report; external auditor attestation; control testing workpapers

key_requirements.text: Establish and maintain disclosure controls and procedures to ensure timely and accurate reporting
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; restatements; D&O liability
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Disclosure committee charters; meeting minutes; controls inventory; gap remediation tracking

key_requirements.text: Maintain audit committee independence — all members must be independent directors
key_requirements.consequence: Exchange delisting; SEC investigation; investor litigation
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board composition records; independence declarations; committee charter

key_requirements.text: Retain all audit-related records for seven years and prohibit destruction during investigations (Section 802)
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal obstruction charges; evidence spoliation sanctions
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Records retention policy; legal hold procedures; destruction logs; storage certificates

key_requirements.text: Implement and protect whistleblower protections for employees reporting suspected fraud
key_requirements.consequence: Civil liability; DOL enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Whistleblower policy; hotline records; non-retaliation training; investigation logs

regulations.penalties: Up to $5 million in fines and 20 years imprisonment for securities fraud

regulations.name: Executive Order 14028 - Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity
regulations.authority: U.S. Office of Management and Budget (OMB), CISA, NIST
regulations.summary: U.S. presidential executive order mandating sweeping cybersecurity improvements across the federal government, catalyzing Zero Trust architecture, SBOM requirements, and software supply chain security.

key_requirements.text: Federal agencies must adopt Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) following NIST SP 800-207
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Software vendors selling to the federal government must provide a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) and encryption for federal systems within 180 days
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence

key_requirements.text: Establish a Cyber Safety Review Board (CSRB) to review significant cyber incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Mandate use of endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions across federal agencies
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks

key_requirements.text: Standardize cybersecurity incident playbooks across federal agencies
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors).
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

regulations.penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors)

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection Law
regulations.authority: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC)
regulations.summary: China's first comprehensive personal information protection law, drawing inspiration from the GDPR while incorporating distinctive Chinese characteristics. Imposes strict cross-border data transfer restrictions and data localization requirements.

key_requirements.text: Obtain separate, specific consent for each personal information processing purpose; consent must be voluntary and unambiguous
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 5% of prior-year revenue; business suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consent records; consent capture mechanisms; withdrawal records; consent management platform

key_requirements.text: Conduct Personal Information Protection Impact Assessments (PIPIAs) before high-risk processing including sensitive data, automated decisions, or cross-border transfers
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; processing orders; suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: PIPIA report templates; completed PIPIA records; review by Person-in-Charge

key_requirements.text: Designate a Person-in-Charge (PIC) for personal information protection within the organisation
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; regulatory inspection
key_requirements.evidence_guide: PIC appointment letter; PIC responsibilities documentation; reporting structure

key_requirements.text: For cross-border transfers: obtain prior CAC approval, pass a security assessment, or use standard contract (PIC); restricted transfers of "important data"
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 5% revenue; data flow injunctions; business suspension
key_requirements.evidence_guide: CAC approval certificates; security assessment report; executed SCCs; transfer records

key_requirements.text: Notify individuals and competent authority within prescribed timeline following personal information security incidents
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; regulatory investigation; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident notification log; CAC/local authority submission receipts; individual notification records

regulations.penalties: Up to RMB 50 million (approx. USD 7 million) or 5% of prior year's annual turnover

regulations.name: Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados (Brazilian General Data Protection Law)
regulations.authority: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados (ANPD)
regulations.summary: Brazil's comprehensive data protection law, heavily influenced by the GDPR. Applies to any processing of personal data performed in Brazil or directed at individuals in Brazil.

key_requirements.text: Establish a legal basis for each personal data processing activity from the 10 LGPD legal bases
key_requirements.consequence: Fines up to 2% of Brazil revenues (max R$50M per violation); suspension of processing
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data processing register showing legal basis per activity; legitimate interest assessments

key_requirements.text: Designate a Data Protection Officer (Encarregado) and publish their contact details
key_requirements.consequence: ANPD enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: DPO appointment record; contact details on company website; ANPD notification

key_requirements.text: Notify ANPD and data subjects of security incidents likely to cause significant harm within a reasonable period
key_requirements.consequence: Fines; ANPD orders; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident notification log; ANPD submission records; individual notification evidence

regulations.penalties: Up to R$50 million (~USD 10 million) per violation or 2% of Brazilian revenue

regulations.name: Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA) / National Data Management Office (NDMO)
regulations.summary: Saudi Arabia's first comprehensive data protection law establishing rights for individuals and obligations for organizations processing personal data of individuals in Saudi Arabia.

key_requirements.text: Obtain explicit consent for processing personal data or rely on alternative legal bases
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Notify data subjects of processing purposes and their rights
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Respect individual rights: access, correction, erasure, and objection
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Report data breaches to SDAIA and affected individuals within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Transfer Impact Assessments before cross-border transfers
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Store data relating to Saudi citizens or residents within Saudi Arabia (data localization)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer for high-risk processing activities
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks

regulations.penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations

regulations.name: Protection of Privacy Law and Privacy Protection Regulations (Data Security)
regulations.authority: Privacy Protection Authority (PPA)
regulations.summary: Israel's foundational data protection framework combining the Protection of Privacy Law (1981) with the comprehensive 2017 Data Security Regulations, adding prescriptive security requirements including database classification, security officer requirements, risk assessments, and breach notification obligations.

key_requirements.text: Register databases containing personal information with the Registrar of Databases (PPA)
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Classify data sensitivity level and implement proportionate security controls
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Database Security Officer for medium and high-security databases
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual internal audits and risk assessments
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules

key_requirements.text: Report severe security incidents to PPA and affected individuals within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Implement access controls, logging, and user authorization procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Inadequate access controls can lead to data breaches and regulatory penalties. Penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Access control policy; user provisioning records; privileged access review logs; authentication configuration evidence

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Obtain consent for data processing; explicit consent for sensitive data
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Honor ARCO rights - Access, Rectification, Cancellation, Opposition - within 20 business days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Sensitive data (health, biometric, racial origin, beliefs) requires explicit consent
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Implement appropriate security measures based on data volume and sensitivity
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs

key_requirements.text: Notify INAI and affected individuals of data security breaches
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Sign data processing agreements with third-party processors
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Designate a data protection department or person responsible for compliance
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

regulations.penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations

regulations.name: Ecuador Organic Law on Personal Data Protection
regulations.authority: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales
regulations.summary: Ecuador's comprehensive data protection law closely modeled on the GDPR, establishing rights for data subjects and obligations for controllers and processors. Applies to any organization processing personal data of individuals located in Ecuador.

key_requirements.text: Lawful basis for processing required - consent, contractual necessity, legitimate interests, legal obligation
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Data subject rights: access, rectification, deletion, portability, objection, restriction, and right not to be subject to automated decisions
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security configuration baselines; encryption certificates; vulnerability scan reports; penetration test results; patch management logs

key_requirements.text: Notify the Superintendencia of data breaches within 72 hours
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Delegate (DPO equivalent) for large-scale processing
key_requirements.consequence: Lack of designated responsibility can result in personal liability for senior management. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks

key_requirements.text: Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules

key_requirements.text: Cross-border data transfer restrictions with adequate protection requirement
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Privacy by design and privacy by default principles
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Board/committee minutes; appointment letters; terms of reference; senior management sign-off records; governance frameworks

regulations.summary: U.S. federal law placing parents in control over the online collection of personal information from children under 13. key_requirements.text: Post a clear and comprehensive privacy policy describing information collection practices

key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA.

regulations.summary: SEC rules requiring public companies to disclose material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days on Form 8-K, and to provide annual disclosures (Form 10-K) about cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and board governance.

key_requirements.text: Disclose material cybersecurity incidents on Form 8-K within 4 business days of determining materiality
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; D&O liability; securities fraud claims

key_requirements.text: Annually disclose cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and governance in Form 10-K

key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; shareholder litigation

regulations.penalties: SEC enforcement actions; material misstatements in public filings subject to securities fraud liability; penalties up to $5 million per violation

regulations.name: New York DFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) — 2023 Amendment
regulations.authority: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)
regulations.summary: The New York Department of Financial Services cybersecurity regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) imposes prescriptive security requirements on all DFS-licensed entities. Originally effective March 2017, a major amendment effective November 1, 2023 substantially expanded requirements: mandatory multi-factor authentication for all systems, 24-hour incident notification to NYDFS, enhanced CISO requirements, annual penetration testing, vulnerability management, and board-level cybersecurity accountability.

key_requirements.text: Establish and maintain a cybersecurity programme based on an annual risk assessment
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; consent orders; fines; licence actions
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Annual risk assessment report; cybersecurity programme documentation; board approval

key_requirements.text: Designate a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) responsible for cybersecurity programme
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; programme deficiency finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: CISO appointment; annual CISO board report; role responsibilities

key_requirements.text: Implement multi-factor authentication for remote access and privileged account access
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; consent orders
key_requirements.evidence_guide: MFA deployment records; privileged access inventory; authentication policy

key_requirements.text: Notify DFS within 72 hours of a cybersecurity event that has a reasonable likelihood of materially affecting operations or involving NPI
key_requirements.consequence: DFS enforcement; reputational damage
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident log; DFS notification receipts; incident classification methodology

key_requirements.text: Conduct annual penetration testing and bi-annual vulnerability assessments
key_requirements.consequence: DFS examination findings; enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Penetration test reports; vulnerability scan results; remediation tracking

regulations.penalties: Up to $250,000 per violation; up to $2,500 per day for extended violations; up to $1 million for serious or systemic violations (2023 amendments)

regulations.name: Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act

regulations.authority: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC)
regulations.summary: Australia's primary privacy law applying to Australian Government agencies and most private-sector organizations with annual turnover over AUD 3 million. key_requirements.text: Notify individuals of what personal information is collected and why at or before the time of collection (APP 5)
key_requirements.consequence: OAIC investigation; civil penalties up to A$50M for serious or repeated breaches

key_requirements.text: Use and disclose personal information only for the primary purpose of collection or a reasonably expected secondary purpose (APP 6)

key_requirements.consequence: Civil penalties up to A$50M; OAIC enforcement
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security risk assessment; technical and organisational controls evidence; incident history

key_requirements.text: Notify OAIC and affected individuals of eligible data breaches as soon as practicable (Notifiable Data Breaches scheme)

regulations.summary: The Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006) is an International Labour Organization (ILO) Convention (C186), often called the 'Seafarers' Bill of Rights.' Administered by the ILO — not the IMO — it consolidates 68 previous ILO maritime standards into a single instrument covering seafarers' employment conditions, working hours, accommodation, health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security. Port state control inspections are coordinated with IMO conventions. Entered into force 20 August 2013; ratified by states representing over 96% of gross tonnage.

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: Install an IMO type-approved Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS) meeting D-2 Performance Standard by the IOPP renewal survey date
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.

key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties.

regulations.name: Singapore Personal Data Protection Act 2012
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC)
regulations.summary: Singapore's primary data protection law governing the collection, use, disclosure, and care of personal data. key_requirements.text: Obtain consent and notify individuals of purposes before or at the time of collecting personal data
key_requirements.consequence: Financial penalty up to S$1M per contravention
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Consent records; consent capture UI evidence; purpose notification records

key_requirements.text: Protect personal data with reasonable security arrangements against unauthorised access, collection, use, disclosure, or disposal

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Security policy; risk assessment; encryption evidence; access control logs

key_requirements.consequence: Financial penalty; PDPC enforcement action
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Breach assessment records; PDPC notification receipts; individual notification evidence

key_requirements.text: Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) and make their business contact details publicly available

regulations.penalties: Up to SGD 1 million or 10% of annual Singapore turnover (whichever is higher) for significant breaches

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.

key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, objection, and data portability
key_requirements.consequence: Absence of required technical safeguards may result in enforcement notices and fines. Penalties: Administrative fines up to TRY 1 million; criminal penalties including imprisonment under Turkish Penal Code.

regulations.summary: Switzerland's revised Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP, revDSG), effective September 2023, modernises Swiss data protection law to align with GDPR standards while maintaining Swiss-specific elements. Introduces privacy by design and default, mandatory breach notification, data protection impact assessments, and enhanced individual rights.

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Criminal fines up to CHF 250,000 for intentional violations; unlike GDPR, penalties are on individuals not organisations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules

key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Administrative fines significantly increased in 2023 — up to RUB 500 million for data breaches; website blocking for non-compliance with localisation.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

regulations.summary: Ukraine's Law on Protection of Personal Data (No. 2297-VI) governs the collection, processing, and storage of personal data. Aligns with Council of Europe Convention 108. key_requirements.text: Honour data subject rights: access, correction, blocking, and deletion

regulations.name: Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA)
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) and Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
regulations.summary: The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act prohibits US persons and issuers from bribing foreign government officials to obtain or retain business. It applies to US companies and citizens worldwide, as well as foreign companies listed on US exchanges. The FCPA also includes accounting provisions requiring accurate books and internal controls.

key_requirements.text: Prohibit payments of anything of value to foreign officials to obtain or retain business; apply to all issuers, domestic concerns, and agents acting in the US
key_requirements.consequence: Criminal: corporations up to $2M per count; individuals up to $250K and 5 years imprisonment; unlimited civil penalties
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-bribery policy; gift and hospitality register; third-party payment logs

key_requirements.text: Issuers must maintain books and records that accurately reflect all transactions in reasonable detail (accounting provisions)
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; disgorgement; civil penalties
key_requirements.evidence_guide: GL reconciliation records; internal audit workpapers; financial controls documentation

key_requirements.text: Implement and maintain a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to prevent and detect bribery
key_requirements.consequence: SEC enforcement; deferred prosecution agreements
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Internal controls assessment; SOX 404 mapping; control testing results

key_requirements.text: Conduct adequate due diligence on all third parties (agents, consultants, distributors) acting on behalf of the company in foreign markets
key_requirements.consequence: Successor liability in M&A; prosecution for wilful blindness
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party DDQ responses; background check reports; red flag escalation records

key_requirements.text: Train all employees and third-party agents on FCPA requirements, anti-bribery policy, and escalation procedures
key_requirements.consequence: Reduced mitigation credit in DOJ/SEC investigations
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; content; assessment results; refresher schedule

regulations.penalties: Corporate fines up to twice the benefit obtained; individuals up to USD 5 million fine and 5 years imprisonment; SEC can impose disgorgement and civil penalties

regulations.name: UK Bribery Act 2010
regulations.authority: Serious Fraud Office (SFO) and Crown Prosecution Service (CPS)
regulations.summary: The UK Bribery Act 2010 is one of the world's strictest anti-bribery laws. It criminalises active and passive bribery, bribery of foreign public officials, and — uniquely — the failure of commercial organisations to prevent bribery by associated persons. The 'adequate procedures' defence requires organisations to implement genuine anti-bribery controls.

key_requirements.text: Commercial organisations must have "adequate procedures" in place to prevent bribery by associated persons
key_requirements.consequence: Unlimited fine for organisation; up to 10 years imprisonment for individuals
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-bribery policy; six-step adequate procedures documentation; board commitment evidence

key_requirements.text: Prohibit offering, promising, or giving a financial or other advantage to induce improper performance
key_requirements.consequence: Unlimited fine; 10 years imprisonment; SFO prosecution
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Gifts register; pre-approval records; hospitality log; escalation log

key_requirements.text: Conduct a proportionate risk assessment of bribery risks across business activities and geographies
key_requirements.consequence: Adequate procedures defence unavailable without documented risk assessment
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Bribery risk assessment report; country risk ratings; annual review records

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence on individuals and entities who perform services on behalf of the organisation
key_requirements.consequence: Prosecution of organisation for failure to prevent bribery by associated person
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party due diligence policy; DDQ responses; background checks; ongoing monitoring

regulations.penalties: Unlimited corporate fines; individuals up to 10 years imprisonment; debarment from public contracts

regulations.name: France Sapin II Law — Anti-Corruption and Transparency
regulations.authority: Agence Française Anticorruption (AFA)
regulations.summary: France's Sapin II Law imposes mandatory anti-corruption compliance programmes on large companies (500+ employees, €100M+ turnover). Enforced by the Agence Française Anticorruption (AFA), it requires a specific eight-pillar compliance programme. AFA can conduct controls and impose sanctions without criminal proceedings.

key_requirements.text: Entities with 500+ employees and €100M+ turnover must implement an anti-corruption compliance programme (8 prescribed measures)
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; fines up to €200K for individuals and €1M for entities; compulsory compliance programme order
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption programme documentation; all 8 measure implementation evidence

key_requirements.text: Establish a risk mapping identifying and prioritising corruption risks exposure
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; programme inadequacy finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk mapping report; risk assessment methodology; annual review records

key_requirements.text: Implement a secure and confidential whistleblowing alert system
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; Sapin II non-compliance finding
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Alert system documentation; confidentiality procedures; investigation handling records

key_requirements.text: Conduct due diligence on third parties (clients, suppliers, intermediaries) presenting corruption risk
key_requirements.consequence: AFA enforcement; programme inadequacy
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Third-party due diligence procedures; DDQ responses; risk-based screening records

regulations.penalties: AFA injunctions; personal liability of executive: fines up to EUR 200,000; corporate fines up to EUR 1 million; judicial public compliance monitoring

key_requirements.consequence: Insufficient staff training undermines compliance and may be cited in regulatory findings. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments

key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.

key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Training completion records; awareness programme materials; staff acknowledgement logs; competency assessments

key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Corporate fine of not less than 10 times the value of the gratification or MYR 1 million (whichever is higher); individual imprisonment up to 20 years.

regulations.authority: Italian Judicial Authorities (Public Prosecutor's Offices)
regulations.summary: Italy's Legislative Decree 231/2001 establishes administrative liability for legal entities for certain crimes (including corruption, fraud, market manipulation, environmental crimes) committed by directors, employees, or persons under their control. regulations.authority: European Commission — Directorate-General for Taxation and Customs Union (DG TAXUD)
regulations.summary: EU Import Control System 2 (ICS2) is a phased customs pre-arrival safety and security programme replacing ICS1. It requires economic operators to file Entry Summary Declarations (ENS) with pre-loading/pre-arrival data enabling customs risk analysis before goods arrive in the EU. Phase 3 (full scope for all maritime/air/road) is effective March 2025.

key_requirements.text: File Entry Summary Declarations (ENS) with accurate pre-loading data before goods depart for EU
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Provide minimum dataset at pre-loading; full dataset at pre-arrival (PLACI obligations)
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Postal and express carriers must file ENS data before loading in non-EU origin countries
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: All economic operators in the supply chain may be required to contribute ENS data
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Respond to 'Do Not Load' or 'Authorisation Needed' messages from EU customs within specified timeframes
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to notify may result in regulatory fines and enforcement action. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Incident response plan; breach notification logs; supervisor authority submission records; post-incident review reports

key_requirements.text: Maintain data accuracy and completeness — use of EU Customs trader portal or APIs
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Implement ICS2 trader portal access or integrate via certified third-party software
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Economic operators must register in EU Customs Trader Portal and hold EORI number
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

regulations.penalties: Member state-imposed customs penalties for late, incomplete, or inaccurate ENS filings; potential shipment delays and customs examinations

regulations.name: Ocean Shipping Reform Act of 2022 (OSRA)
regulations.authority: Federal Maritime Commission (FMC)
regulations.summary: The Ocean Shipping Reform Act of 2022 strengthens the Shipping Act of 1984 to address unfair practices by ocean common carriers. It provides new FMC enforcement tools, requires carriers to publish service contracts, prohibits unreasonable refusals of export cargo bookings, and mandates transparency in detention and demurrage charges.

key_requirements.text: Ocean carriers must maintain public tariffs and service contracts on file with FMC
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on ocean carriers unreasonably refusing to negotiate service contracts with exporters
key_requirements.consequence: Unlawful data transfers or inadequate vendor contracts expose the organisation to fines. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Vendor contracts/DPAs; third-party risk assessments; supplier due diligence records; data transfer agreements

key_requirements.text: Prohibition on unreasonable refusal of requests for vessel space for US exports
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Ocean carriers must provide detailed billing for detention and demurrage charges
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Detention and demurrage charges must be just and reasonable — FMC can review and refund excess
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules

key_requirements.text: Common carriers must establish accessible complaint procedures for shippers
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.text: FMC has enhanced investigative powers and can initiate enforcement without complaint
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Shipping exchanges (non-vessel operators) must register and comply with regulations
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

regulations.penalties: FMC civil penalties up to USD 78,121 per violation; injunctive relief; cease and desist orders

regulations.name: Corporate Transparency Act — Beneficial Ownership Information Reporting
regulations.authority: Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) — U.S. Treasury
regulations.summary: The Corporate Transparency Act requires most US domestic and foreign companies registered to do business in the US to report their beneficial owners (persons owning 25%+ or exercising substantial control) to FinCEN. Note: as of March 2025, FinCEN temporarily exempted domestic US companies pending regulatory review.

key_requirements.text: Report beneficial ownership information (BOI) to FinCEN — name, DOB, address, and ID document for each beneficial owner
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications

key_requirements.text: Existing companies formed before 2024 had a reporting deadline of January 1, 2025 (domestic companies currently exempt pending review)
key_requirements.consequence: Failure to assess risks may constitute a compliance breach attracting regulatory action. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Risk register; DPIA/PIA records; internal/external audit reports; risk treatment plans; review schedules

key_requirements.text: New companies formed after 2024 must report within 30 days of formation
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Data inventory/classification register; processing records; data lifecycle policy; records of lawful basis

key_requirements.text: Report changes to beneficial ownership or company information within 30 days
key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications

key_requirements.text: Foreign companies registered to do business in the US must report
key_requirements.consequence: Missing or inadequate policies may be cited in regulatory audits and lead to sanctions. Penalties: Civil penalty of USD 500 per day for ongoing violations; criminal penalty of up to USD 10,000 and 2 years imprisonment for wilful violations.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Policy register; Records of Processing Activities; consent management records; privacy notices; data subject request logs

key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications

regulations.name: Brazil CVM Resolution 193 — ESG Reporting (ISSB Standards)
regulations.authority: Securities and Exchange Commission of Brazil (CVM)
regulations.summary: Brazil's CVM Resolution 193 requires publicly listed companies to report on sustainability matters using ISSB Standards (IFRS S1 and S2), making Brazil an early adopter of mandatory climate and sustainability disclosure aligned with international standards. Mandatory for listed companies reporting on fiscal year 2026.

key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance may result in criminal prosecution, fines, and reputational damage. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
key_requirements.evidence_guide: Anti-corruption policy; gift register; financial control documentation; internal audit reports; compliance certifications

key_requirements.consequence: Non-compliance with data governance obligations may attract regulatory fines and orders. Penalties: CVM administrative sanctions for material non-disclosure; reputational and investor relations impact.
